thread.title: Richmond Public Schools prepares for potential effects of Trump administration
thread.title_full: Richmond Public Schools prepares for potential effects of Trump administration

author: ANNA BRYSON and EM HOLTER Richmond Times-Dispatch
title: Richmond Public Schools prepares for potential effects of Trump administration
text:
As school leaders across the U.S. prepare for
[the incoming Trump administration's potential impact on education](main/preview/site/-/news/state-regional/education/virginia-trump-department-of-education-youngkin/article_1ced232e-9e24-11ef-a5c7-5f918f83a227.html), the superintendent of Virginia’s capital city is getting ready for what he sees as the two most immediate potential changes: mass deportations and the end of federally funded school lunches.
In Richmond, where
[82% of voters cast their ballots for Vice President Kamala Harris](https://www.vpap.org/electionresults/20241105/potus/), about a quarter of the city’s 22,000 public school students are Hispanic. A significant percentage of those students are recent immigrants, according to Richmond Public Schools Superintendent Jason Kamras, and some of them are undocumented.
President-elect Donald Trump has vowed to start the largest deportation effort in U.S. history.
People are also reading…
“There's a lot of fear in our recent immigrant community about deportations," Kamras said on Friday in a meeting with reporters and editors at the Richmond Times-Dispatch."
"It has caused a great deal of anxiety. Obviously, if something like that were to occur, it would deeply impact our students,” he said.
“We are beginning to think through, what would protections in particular for our undocumented students look like. How do we make sure that they understand that school is a sanctuary for them?”
Project 2025, a project of policy proposals led by the Heritage Foundation and much of it crafted by individuals with ties to Trump’s first administration, includes a call to eliminate a program that provides funding for school meals in low-income schools and districts. (Trump's campaign and Trump himself
[sought to distance themselves from Project 2025](https://apnews.com/article/project-2025-trump-harris-election-heritage-foundation-9183cf4c36c293e11b59847189d26a87).)
The
[Community Eligibility Provision](https://www.fns.usda.gov/cn/cep) is provided to schools or divisions where a significant number of students are in foster care or Head Start; are homeless; are migrant; or are living in households that participate in federal assistance programs. RPS provides free school meals to its students because of this federal program, and some schools in Henrico and Chesterfield counties provide free meals to all students.
Kamras: Officials thinking about funding school meals locally
State Sen. Danica Roem, D-Prince William, who has been
[an advocate of feeding schoolchildren](https://richmond.com/news/state-and-regional/govt-and-politics/legislation-aims-to-provide-free-school-meals-for-all-virginia-students/article_12528f86-95c9-11ed-8e69-9b3dec8af939.html) at no cost to their families, said she has been concerned about school meals since the day after the election.
“Eliminating the Community Eligibility Provision would result in millions of dollars of school meal debt returning and recurring annually across the commonwealth,” Roem said. “I would remind the incoming administration that in the 9th and 6th Congressional districts" in Southwest Virginia and along the state's western spine from Winchester to Roanoke, "where they earned their highest percentage of the vote — entire school districts, county after county, have no school meals debt because of CEP.”
Kamras, who has been RPS superintendent since 2018, said the administration is beginning to think through what it would cost to continue to provide meals for students if there was a rollback and how the division could come up with the money, whether through local funding, philanthropy or partnership with other organizations. It would cost millions, he said.
“We delivered millions of meals during the pandemic,” Kamras said. “So we'll have to figure this one out, too, if necessary.”
One-fifth of funding comes from feds
The president-elect has nominated Linda McMahon, who founded World Wrestling Entertainment, to be education secretary. McMahon led the Small Business Administration from 2017 to 2019 during Trump’s first term.
Trump’s
[Agenda 47 plan](https://www.donaldjtrump.com/platform), which includes his administration’s goals for public education, proposes a number of changes, including cutting federal funding to schools that teach “inappropriate racial, sexual or political” content; rolling back Title IX regulations that protect transgender youth; and creating a credentialing body to certify teachers “who embrace patriotic values and support the American way of life.”
It’s unclear what criteria the administration would use to target schools to cut their funding. About one-fifth of RPS’s budget comes from federal funding.
The division has been consistent in its support of transgender students. One day after Gov. Glenn Youngkin’s education department released new model transgender policies that rolled back some protections afforded to trans students under the previous administration, Kamras publicly said he would recommend that the School Board maintain the division’s current policies on transgender students and reject the state's new guidance.
“We’re preparing as best as we can," Kamras said, "and anything that undermines either the finances or the stability and mental health for our kids and our families, then I will be very vocal about it and we will fight very, very hard."
Here are the people Trump has picked for key positions so far
President-elect Donald Trump
Susie Wiles, White House Chief of Staff
Marco Rubio, Secretary of State
Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Defense
Pam Bondi, Attorney General
Kristi Noem, Secretary of Homeland Security
Doug Burgum, Secretary of the Interior
Robert F. Kennedy Jr., Secretary of Health and Human Services
Sean Duffy, Secretary of Transportation
Chris Wright, Secretary of Energy
Linda McMahon, Secretary of Education
Howard Lutnick, Secretary of Commerce
Doug Collins, Secretary of Veterans Affairs
Karoline Leavitt, White House press secretary
Tulsi Gabbard, National Intelligence Director
John Ratcliffe, Central Intelligence Agency Director
Lee Zeldin, Environmental Protection Agency Administrator
Brendan Carr, Chairman of the Federal Communications Commission
Elise Stefanik, Ambassador to the United Nations
Matt Whitaker, Ambassador to NATO
Pete Hoekstra, Ambassador to Canada
Mike Huckabee, Ambassador to Israel
Steven Witkoff, Special Envoy to the Middle East
Mike Waltz, National Security Adviser
Stephen Miller, Deputy Chief of Staff for Policy
Tom Homan, ‘Border Czar’
Dr. Mehmet Oz, Centers for Medicaid and Medicare Services administrator
Elon Musk and Vivek Ramaswamy to advise White House on government efficiency
